data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0954 ET - Cryptocurrencies had a down week, with total market cap off 1.8% in the past 24 hours and down roughly 13% in the past 30 days, according to data from CoinMarketCap. In the past week, the market seems to have been selling off following President Trump's announcement of a strategic bitcoin reserve--including a crypto summit held at the White House on Friday. Markets appear underwhelmed with the news out of Washington, but the fall of bitcoin to a recent low of roughly $82,000 may soon turn around, says Nigel Green of the deVere Group. "The market's reaction betrays a short-sightedness that will likely be corrected as investors digest the broader implications of the move," says Green in a note. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0939 ET - U.K. government plans to raise defense spending are boosting the performance of the country's defense stocks, driving them to perform better than U.S. tech stocks, XTB research director Kathleen Brooks says in a note. "Rolls Royce is the best performing stock on the FTSE 100 for 2025 so far, as U.K. defense firms continue to outpace big tech in the U.S." she says. The U.K. multinational aerospace and defense company's stock price is up by 30% since the start of 2025. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0918 ET - Volatility in cryptocurrencies is likely to remain elevated in the absence of clear guidance on President Trump's plans for a strategic bitcoin reserve, Deutsche Bank's Marion Laboure says in a note. Key parameters such as the timeline for execution, funding mechanisms, and asset allocation remain uncertain, she says. The market remains watchful of developments, weighing the potential for cryptocurrency gains if the plan gathers momentum and the risk of losses if the plan stalls or remains a largely symbolic move, she says. Bitcoin last trades flat at $83,013, according to LSEG. It reached a one-week low of $80,085 overnight.(renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0901 ET - Short-term headwinds are likely to continue weighing on PageGroup's stock as a market recovery isn't expected until 2025's second half, Deutsche Bank analyst Steve Woolf says in a research note. The U.K. recruiter is facing weak market conditions, especially in Europe, where France, Germany and the U.K. represent 40% of group fees, Woolf says. The company's earnings remain under pressure in the near term, he adds, noting the bank has cut its earnings estimates for 2025 and 2026. Notwithstanding the challenges, PageGroup has a strong cash position and may return more capital to shareholders later in the year, he adds. Shares fall 1.6% to 326.20 pence. (maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

0834 ET - Galderma's guidance for the current year is in line with expectations, but carries some uncertainty, RBC Capital Markets analysts Alistair Campbell and Charles Weston said in a note. The Swiss skincare maker expects the first quarter to be subdued, with an acceleration through the year. "We think this brings some risk to the outlook, which may not be resolved until the second half of the year," the analysts say. Galderma targets net sales growth between 10% and 12% at constant currency in 2025. It also forecasts a core Ebitda margin of around 23% at constant exchange rates. RBC Capital Markets downgrades the stock to sector perform from outperform. Shares trade 4% lower at 95.48 Swiss francs. (andrea.figueras@wsj.com)

0826 ET - Long-term investors willing to wait for strong cash returns might find PageGroup appealing despite current market challenges, RBC Capital Markets analyst Karl Green says in a note. The London-listed recruiter is a good pick for those willing to wait out the current downturn, especially given its strong balance sheet, resilient free cash flow and commitment to shareholder returns, Green notes. As part of its aggressive shareholder returns policy, the company aims to return nearly its entire market capitalization--which currently amounts to 1.22 billion pounds--to investors by the end of the decade, mostly via special dividends. The analyst expects PageGroup to be worth 445 pence a share in a normal market cycle. Shares fall 1.1% to 327.80 pence. (maitane.sardon@wsj.com)

0821 ET - The Canadian dollar faces further falls in the coming months even after President Trump postponed 25% tariffs on many imports from Canada for a month, ING analysts say in a note. Even without a flat 25% tariff, Canadian goods are being selectively targeted by the U.S., while reciprocal tariffs are looming, they say. Canada should be "disproportionately affected" by tariffs due to its high export volumes to the U.S., they say. With the dollar likely to recover this summer, USD/CAD could trade above 1.45, they say. USD/CAD rises 0.2% to 1.4404. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0802 ET - Deliveroo's Hong Kong assets could become more profitable under rival Delivery Hero's Foodpanda, potentially boosting pricing power and profitability, says Mwb research analyst Alexander Zienkowicz. Deliveroo's exit from Hong Kong and the sale of some of its assets to Foodpanda will strengthen the latter's market position in an increasingly competitive market, he writes in a note to investors. KeeTa, the international arm of China's biggest food delivery app, Meituan, dominates the Hong Kong market with 43% of order volume, Zienkowicz notes. The analyst says he doesn't expect Delivery Hero to overpay. Shares in Deliveroo are up 2% at 1.28 pounds, while shares in Delivery Hero are down 3% at 27.96 euros.(cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0757 ET - BP's share price reaction to the oil major's new strategy shows an underwhelmed market, AJ Bell investment director Russ Mould writes. BP had announced last month that it would pivot back to fossil fuels in a bid to revive its flagging share price. The energy company's strategy update is aimed at winning over investors. This includes activist hedge fund Elliott Management, which recently took a stake in BP with a view to pushing for significant changes. In mid-April, BP will host its annual general meeting and will be sitting uneasily in case it gets an aggressive letter from Elliott, Mould writes. "The meeting itself will be a test of the current management's ability to resist outside pressure and pursue its own course," Mould adds. Shares closed at 436.90 pence on the day before the strategy announcement, and are currently up 1.5% at 419.45 pence.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0752 ET - German industry in January posted the strongest monthly output increase since August, though December's production losses were only partially offset and the volatile auto industry was the key driver of overall output, HSBC's Anja Sabine Heimann says. Indeed, with fragile orders, where underlying demand has started to weaken again, and persistent tariff uncertainty, no sustainable recovery in production is in sight, she says in a note. However, by contrast, the medium-term outlook has improved through the proposals of the incoming governing coalition. For industry, the CDU and SPD say they intend to cut bureaucracy costs, reduce electricity taxes, build new gas-fired power plants and present more subsidies for energy-intensive industries, Heimann notes. (edward.frankl@wsj.com)

0734 ET - The U.S. is in a unique position when it comes to global arms exports, with a share of 43%, more than four times as much as France, the next largest exporter, says Mathew George, program director at the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute (SIPRI). "The USA continues to be the supplier of choice for advanced long-range strike capabilities like combat aircraft," George says. For the first time in two decades, the largest share of U.S. arms exports in 2020-24 went to Europe, at 35%, instead of the Middle East (33%), according to a SIPRI report published Monday. Saudi Arabia was the top single recipient of U.S. arms, representing 12% of U.S. arms exports, the report states. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

0731 ET - Shares in London-listed miners fell on Chinese deflation fears and the prospect of a recession in the U.S. In European late morning trade, Copper miner Antofagasta was the largest faller. It's shares traded down 3.6% at 1778.50 pence, while Anglo American fell 2.7% to 2317.50 pence. Silver and gold miner Fresnillo fell 1.5% to 836.00 pence, and Glencore fell 0.6% at 317.10 pence. Data showed that China's inflation rate was negative in February and domestic demand was weak, provoking concerns given the threat tariffs could pose to the country's exports, AJ Bell's Russ Mould writes. Separately, President Trump on Sunday refused to rule out a recession in the U.S. this year. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

data.title: Assura to Accept KKR's $2.08 Billion Takeover Offer — Update
data.description:
By Michael Susin

Assura said it started discussions on an improved takeover offer from the consortium formed by KKR and Stonepeak Partners that values the company at 1.61 billion pounds ($2.08 billion).

The London-listed property business on Monday said its board would recommend the deal if the 49.4 pence-a-share bid becomes a firm offer, and allowed the consortium to complete a due diligence process.

The consortium's offer represents a premium of nearly 32% to the shares' closing price of 37.4 pence on Feb. 13, and a 2.9% increase from KKR's previous proposal of 48 pence a share that was rejected on Feb. 18. This is the fifth indicative nonbinding proposal from KKR to the board of Assura.

Under the deal, Assura shareholders would keep the declared quarterly dividend of 0.84 pence a share and receive cash consideration of 48.56 pence a share at closing.

"The consortium of KKR and Stonepeak, both long-term infrastructure investors, recognizes that Assura's leading platform and portfolio are important social infrastructure assets for the U.K., and has indicated its intention to deploy further capital to the portfolio to continue its growth," the company said.

Assura added that it rejected an offer from Primary Health Properties regarding a possible all-share combination of Assura and PHP, as it considered KKR's offer to be more attractive because it provides cash at a significantly higher value per share and with materially less risk.

In a separate statement, Primary Health Properties said it is "considering its position" after confirming that it has made an unsuccessful preliminary approach. PHP added that it didn't have any details around the exchange ratio.

The company said a business combination would boost the scale of both companies' portfolio, reduce costs and realize significant value from synergies.

PHP has until April 7 to either announce a firm intention to make an offer or walk away.

Assura stock rose more than 14% on the news at 46.62 pounds, on track for a record intraday increase.

According to AJ Bell investment director Russ Mould, the current share price is below KKR's latest proposal, suggesting skepticism if the new KKR proposal is a done deal. It also implies the market doesn't believe Primary Health Properties is going to come back with a significantly better offer.

"The 31.9% bid premium is significantly below the 47% average for U.K. takeovers in 2024, meaning Assura's shareholders might feel they aren't being compensated adequately. After all, they would be giving up an investment that could generate much greater returns over time," Mould added.

Write to Michael Susin at michael.susin@wsj.com

data.title: Greece reaffirms commitment to subsea power link to Cyprus
data.description:
Greece reaffirmed on Monday its commitment to building a subsea electric cable planned to link continental Europe to the East Mediterranean, following reports that the project was halted over financial and geopolitical concerns.

Greek power grid operator IPTO is building the Great Sea Interconnector (GSI) cable, which will link transmission networks of Europe to Cyprus in a project costing 1.9 billion euros ($2.12 billion), and later stretch to Israel through the Mediterranean Sea.

But progress has been delayed by a long-standing dispute between Greece and Turkey, both NATO allies, over the limits of their continental shelves in the Mediterranean Sea.

Vessels leased by Nexans to carry out research in sea waters off the island of Crete to help determine the route of the cable were allegedly forced to leave earlier than scheduled this year, after Turkey challenged Greece's jurisdiction over the area.

The link has been a source of unusual disagreement between Greece and Cyprus, two historical allies, with Cyprus seeking assurances on the project's viability.

Shares in French cable maker Nexans fell last Thursday after a Greek newspaper reported that IPTO missed a payment until further notice. Nexans later said it had received substantial payments that allowed it to continue production of the cable and that it would respect the project's contractual obligations.

Asked about the fate of the link, Greek government spokesperson Pavlos Marinakis told a bi-weekly news conference on Monday. "I reaffirm all stakeholders' commitment to the successful conclusion of this infrastructure project of strategic importance."

Greek, Cypriot and Israeli foreign ministers are due to meet in Athens on Thursday to discuss the issue.

data.title: Riyadh Cables Posts Higher FY24 Net Profit, Revenue
data.description:
Riyadh Cables Group (SASE:4142) said Monday profit and revenue for 2024 grew year over year.

Net profit for the 12 months ended Dec. 31, 2024, was 816.9 million Saudi riyals, compared with 518.5 million riyals earlier. EPS moved to 5.46 riyals from 3.46 riyals.

The Saudi Arabia-listed cable manufacturer's revenue was 9.01 billion riyals, compared with 7.83 billion riyals a year ago.

data.title: Dave Board Authorizes $50 Million Share Buyback Plan
data.description:
Dave said Monday its board has approved a repurchase program for up to $50 million of its Class A shares.

The program does not have a fixed expiration date and will be executed from time to time based on market conditions, liquidity and other considerations, the company said.

Dave shares were 0.5% lower in recent trading.

data.title: China Slips Back Into Price Deflation in February
data.description:
Aggravating concerns that the Chinese economy is still struggling to recover from the pandemic era, the nation's major price indices slipped into deflation in February.

China's consumer price index (CPI) fell 0.7% year on year in February, the National Bureau of Statistics (NBS) reported Sunday.

For the first two months of the year, the CPI was down 0.1% on year, and the index declined 0.2% in February from January, added the NBS.

Helping to cool prices, food prices declined 3.3% on year in February.

The CPI-core, that strips out certain food and energy bills, fell 0.1% on year, according to official figures.

China officials said the most-recent deflation reading was due to the timing of the annual Spring Festival holidays, which in 2024 fell during February.

"Last February, food and service prices increased significantly during the traditional Spring Festival holiday, creating a high base effect that contributed to this year's decline," said NBS statistician Dong Lijuan, reported the state-run Xinhua news service.

China's producer price index (PPI), which measures costs for goods at the factory gate, decreased 2.2% on year in February, the NBS also reported

Possibly indicating industry over-capacity, prices in the ferrous metal smelting and pressing industry fell 10.6% year on year in February.

Beijing and the People's Bank of China have an inflation target of "about 3%" on the nation's CPI, but the nation's inflation rate has been below that target since 2020, and has been below 1% since early 2023.

In particular, China's property markets have struggled since the pandemic era. In February, sales prices of existing homes fell 7.3% on year, reported the China Index Academy, while listed property developers, such as China Evergrande, have recently gone bankrupt.

At the annual "Two Sessions" government meeting held last week in Beijing, high government officials vowed to boost macroeconomic stimulus, to keep the nation's economy on a path to expand by 5% in 2025.

data.title: Cboe to Launch S&P 500 Equal Weight Index Options on April 14
data.description:
Cboe Global Markets (CBOE) said Monday it plans to launch options on the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index on April 14, pending regulatory review.

The cash-settled options will reflect one-tenth the value of the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index, the equal-weight version of the S&P 500 Index, the Cboe said.

The new options are intended to give investors exposure to an index less impacted by potential shifts in concentration and momentum, giving them the ability to hedge against potential swings in the largest S&P 500 stocks, the exchange said.

data.title: Update: Appian Gets Virginia Supreme Court Hearing on Reinstating $2.04 Billion Verdict Against Pegasystems
data.description:
(Updates with company's response in the fifth paragraph and latest stock move in the final paragraph.)

Appian said Friday that the Supreme Court of Virginia agreed to hear its petition to reinstate a jury's $2.04 billion judgment against Pegasystems for trade secret misappropriation.

The court agreed to review four issues raised in the Court of Appeals decision.

Appian sued Pegasystems to protect its intellectual property. A jury found that Pegasystems used a "spy" to get trade secrets, benefiting financially from the misappropriation, Appian said.

The Supreme Court also agreed to review Pegasystems' cross-appeal on trade secret misappropriation, though lower courts rejected its arguments, Appian said.

"We stand firmly by our position that the overturned verdict in this case was a result of a flawed trial on many fronts, including that we were prevented from showing that our software never adopted any of Appian's supposed trade secrets," a Pegasystems spokesperson said in an emailed statement to MT Newswires. "The Appellate Court's 60-page decision was unanimous, and we believe Appian will have to overcome numerous, thorough, and well-reasoned grounds to overturn the appeals court decision."

Shares of Appian were 3% lower in recent Monday trading, and Pegasystems shares were down 4.2%.

data.title: HSBC Upgrades European Stocks on Germany's 'Game-changing' Fiscal Stimulus
data.description:
HSBC Global Research upgraded its rating on European equities, excluding the UK, to overweight from underweight on the back of a "watershed momentum" for the eurozone, primarily driven by an expected fiscal stimulus from Germany.

Analysts see Germany's significant fiscal package as a potential "game-changer," with the country's plan to implement a 500 billion-euro infrastructure spending program, alongside a defense spending boost, viewed as a structural catalyst for eurozone equities, according to a Monday note.

"If the policy measures can pass the Bundestag (which seems likely to us), it could help sustain European equities momentum and see a broadening of performance, particularly among German domestic-related stocks. It also leaves the door open for other countries to follow," HSBC said, noting that infrastructure spending is "arguably a much bigger driver than defense spending given higher multiplier effects."

The research firm, meanwhile, downgraded US stocks to neutral. "It is important to stress that we are not turning negative on US equities - but tactically, we see better opportunities elsewhere for now," analysts added.

data.description: Egypt's core inflation fell down to 10% on year-on-year basis in February, from 22.6% in January, the central bank said on Monday.

data.title: These Stocks Are Moving the Most Today: Tesla, Nvidia, Redfin, DoorDash, MicroStrategy, Robinhood, Oracle, and More — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Joe Woelfel

Stock futures fell sharply Monday after President Donald Trump said he couldn't rule out a recession as the U.S. economy goes through a "period of transition."

These stocks were poised to make moves Monday:

Redfin soared 70% to $9.87 after agreeing to be purchased by Rocket Cos. in a deal that values the real estate listing site at $1.75 billion. Rocket will acquire Redfin for $12.50 a share in the all-stock transaction. The deal is expected to close in the second or third quarter. Rocket fell 14%.

DoorDash rose 3.7% in premarket trading after the online food-delivery company was added to the S&P 500. It will join home goods company Williams-Sonoma, World Wrestling Entertainment owner TKO Group Holdings, and natural gas producer Expand Energy in the index. The changes announced Friday by S&P Dow Jones Indices will take effect before the start of trading on March 24.

Williams-Sonoma rose 1.2% in premarket trading, TKO Group gained 0.3%, and Expand Energy was up 1.7%. The four companies added to the S&P 500 will replace BorgWarner, Teleflex, Celanese, and FMC.

Nvidia fell 2.6% in premarket trading to $109.77 after closing Friday up 1.9%. Coming into Monday, shares of the leading maker of artificial-intelligence chips have dropped 16% in 2025. Worries over economic growth and uncertainties about Trump's trade policies overshadowed news that Nvidia provided support for the building of Foxconn's own large language model with reasoning capabilities.

Tesla declined 3.8% in premarket trading to $252.62. Shares have fallen for seven straight weeks, ever since CEO Elon Musk joined the Trump administration as part of the Department of Government Efficiency. The stock has declined about 38% since Donald Trump's inauguration on Jan. 20. Wedbush analyst Dan Ives wrote Thursday that investors will have to look past Musk-related issues. He rates the shares a Buy and has a price target of $550 on the stock.

MicroStrategy fell 5.1%, Coinbase Global fell 5.2%, and Robinhood Markets was down 7.5% as the crypto-linked stocks followed Bitcoin lower on disappointment over Trump's executive order to create a strategic Bitcoin reserve for the United States. The reserve will be capitalized using Bitcoin obtained through criminal and civil forfeiture proceedings, rather than government buying.

Robinhood, meanwhile, will pay nearly $30 million in restitution and fines for allegedly violating "numerous" rules, brokerage industry self-regulatory organization Finra said late Friday.

Beacon Roofing Supply was up 8.5% after the roofing materials supplier said it was in discussions with QXO about being purchased in a deal valued around $11 billion.

Cognizant Technology Solutions was up 2% after The Wall Street Journal, citing people familiar with the matter, reported activist investor Mantle Ridge has built a stake of more than $1 billion in the tech-services company, believed the shares are undervalued. Cognizant said in a statement it has been constructively engaging with Mantle Ridge, the Journal reported.

DexCom received a warning letter from the Food and Drug Administration following inspections of its facilities in San Diego and Mesa, Ariz. The diabetes devices maker said it doesn't expect a "material impact" from the warning letter to its manufacturing capacity or fiscal-year revenue guidance. The stock fell 3.7%.

BioNTech was down 4% in U.S. trading after fourth-quarter revenue at the vaccine maker fell to $1.19 billion from $1.48 billion a year earlier. The decrease was "primarily driven by lower sales of the company's Covid-19 vaccines due to reduced market demand," the company said.

Earnings reports are expected after the closing bell Monday from Oracle, and Vail Resorts.

Oracle was falling 2.4% in premarket trading ahead of fiscal third-quarter earnings from the enterprise software company. The stock has declined 6.9% this year.

Reports are expected later in the week from Adobe, Lennar, Ferguson Enterprises, Viking Holdings, Dick's Sporting Goods, Ciena, Kohl's, UiPath, SentinelOne, American Eagle Outfitters, DocuSign, Ulta Beauty, Dollar General, Rubrik, D-Wave Quantum, and Li Auto.

Write to Joe Woelfel at joseph.woelfel@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: US yields drop on economic growth fears
data.description:
U.S. Treasury yields fell on Monday after comments by U.S. President Donald Trump on Sunday raised concerns about an impending U.S. recession.

In an interview, Trump declined to predict whether the U.S. could face a recession amid stock market concerns about his tariff actions on Mexico, Canada and China, saying that "there is a period of transition.”

U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said on Friday that the U.S. economy may slow as it transitions away from public spending towards more private spending, calling it a "detox period" needed to reach a more sustainable equilibrium.

“If the occupant in the White House is himself not terribly optimistic about short-term growth expectations, why should the market be optimistic about it?” said Will Compernolle, macro strategist at FHN Financial.

“If they are willing to look through what they see as short-term pain, the detox, then there's an even bigger risk that, after the detox, they don't really have the capability to stop a downturn before it's too late".

The yield on benchmark U.S. 10-year notes was last down 8.2 basis points on the day at 4.236%. The 2-year note (US2YT=RR) yield fell 7.3 basis points to 3.929%.

The spread between two-year and 10-year Treasury yields (US2US10=TWEB) steepened by around one basis point to 31 basis points.

A haphazard implementation of Trump's trade tariffs has increased uncertainty over when and how long levies may be in place, which has added to fears over how they may impact growth and inflation.

Trump on Thursday suspended tariffs of 25% he had imposed last week on most goods from Canada and Mexico. The exemptions for the two largest U.S. trading partners expire on April 2.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, meanwhile, said on Friday that the U.S. central bank is in no rush to resume interest rate cuts, with inflation still "somewhat above" the Fed's 2% target.

February’s jobs report showed that U.S. job growth picked up in February, though the share of workers holding multiple jobs was the highest since the Great Recession.

This week’s main economic focus will be consumer price inflation data for February, due on Wednesday, which follows a much hotter than expected report in January. Producer price data is scheduled for Thursday.

The Treasury will sell $119 billion in coupon-bearing debt this week, including $58 billion in three-year notes on Tuesday, $39 billion in 10-year notes on Wednesday and $22 billion in 30-year bonds on Thursday.

data.title: Canada's Well-Below-Consensus Employment Points to A Bank of Canada Rate Cut Wednesday, Says Rosenberg Research
data.description:
Canada's Labour Force Survey (LFS) Friday surprised to the downside with a big miss, with employment up just 1,100 jobs — below consensus of 20,000 rises, which was already on the low side, said Rosenberg Research.

This confirms the weak labor market picture — lowest job creation number since July 2024 — and reinforces the Babj of Canada's easing bias, which supports a rate cut on Wednesday and beyond, noted Rosenberg Research. BoC rate cut odds for this week's policy meeting have gone from 50% the week before to 76% on Thursday and to 85% Friday.

Indeed, without wholesale trade hiring effects ahead of possible United States tariffs, the report would have shown an outright decline. This reverses the fairly strong employment picture investors saw in Q4 and is consistent with tariff uncertainty tipping Canada's growth downward, pointed out Rosenberg.

Unemployment was steady at 6.6% — well above the neutral unemployment rate of around 5.7%, continuing the excess supply picture. This was below consensus (6.7%) because of a drop in the participation rate to 65.3% from 65.5%, added Rosenberg. This continues to reinforce the BoC's dovish-but-slow approach.

Also showing the weak picture is the full-time employment decline (-20, 000), with the offset all coming from part-time work (+21, 000).

The employment picture varied "wildly" across industries, with wholesale and retail trade up 51,000, which Rosenberh believes partially reflects the impact of possible tariffs.

If wholesale and retail trade were omitted, there would have been a 50,000 job loss. Meanwhile, transportation and warehousing (-23, 000) and manufacturing (-5, 000) both fell, suggesting that the Canadian dollar loonie hasn't declined enough, as these are very currency-sensitive sectors. The goods-producing sector as a whole continued to decline (-20, 000) amid Canada's manufacturing recession, while services were up (+21, 000). Finance, insurance, and real estate (+16, 000) is a rate-sensitive area that continues to be a source of support as the BoC eases. Professional, scientific, and technical services (-33, 000) also saw a large drop.

Total hours worked fell 1.3% month over month due to snowstorms and other seasonal disruptions and were up just 0.5% year over year. This is a weak level consistent with limited business activity and a poor profitability picture.

Wage growth of 3.8% year over was slightly elevated from January's moderate read of 3.5%, but still consistent with a disinflating economy as it is the second-lowest year-over-year pace since May 2022. Like the drop in monthly hours worked, the month-over-month pop in wages is a result of the winter storms in February and should fade, according to Rosenberh. The build-in suggests an annualized employment growth in Q1 of +2.9%, mostly reflecting strong growth in January rather than last month.

Overall, everything in this report points to continued BoC easing.

data.title: Protagonist Therapeutics Shares Rise After Positive Trial Data for Icotronkinra
data.description:
By Chris Wack

Protagonist Therapeutics shares were up 28% to $49.25 after the company reported positive topline results from a Phase 2b study of icotrokinra, conducted by partner Johnson & Johnson.

The biopharmaceutical company said icotrokinra is a targeted oral peptide that selectively blocks the IL-23 receptor in adults with moderately to severely active ulcerative colitis.

Protagonist said the study met its primary endpoint of clinical response in all icotrokinra dose groups evaluated. The study also demonstrated clinically meaningful differences versus placebo in key secondary endpoints of clinical remission, symptomatic remission and endoscopic improvement at week 12.

Icotrokinra had a response rate of 63.5% for patients treated with the highest dose at week 12, versus 27% for placebo. Clinical remission and response rates continued to improve through week 28.

Icotrokinra was well tolerated with the proportions of participants reporting one or more adverse events being similar between the icotrokinra dose groups and the placebo group.

Write to Chris Wack at chris.wack@wsj.com
